sentences.text: It take-- it took me a while.
I started to walk like a baby of eight months, pulling one leg after the other on the floor.
In this time, the Germans had idea.
They took the whole-- OK, meantime it became winter.
It was 1943.
And the winter was that terrible.
And they wanted us-- they-- the whole camp, all the girls, they sent out to a paddock before the camp.
There was nothing there.
And so people-- they was standing there, and some people froze to death or, or lied down.
It was from morning till evening.
The girls who are-- was friendly with me and look after me, so they put me to a-- there was a rock.
They put me there.
They carried me.
First of all, they carried me, because I couldn't walk.
So two strong girls, they hold my arm like that, and I was dangling like a door, because I didn't hear, I didn't see, and I couldn't walk.
So they dangled me there.
They put me to the rock, and from time to time they carried me around so I should not freeze to death to the rock.

And one of the girls, she put tea-- whatever tea it was, but tea, and it was liquidy-- in one-- I don't know where she got-- one bottle, and the bottle she put in a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] -- utensil, you call?
Like a plate, but it was a special plate.
Not from-- not from glass, not from porcelain, but from-- Paper?
No.
Like this.
No.
This is not plastic.
What you call?
No.
Anyway, it was such a plate, a, a round plate which they-- every person supposed to have, and here she put the bottle in.
And because they been so flat, nobody saw that she has there something.
And from time to time, she gave me a little bit of liquid so I should be able to exist.
And the same way on the way back, all the five girl-- four girls in the row were special strong girls, because they knew when they will march strongly, they won't look in, because they march.
So four strong girls was, and I was in the middle.
So 80% of the woman was sent to the gas chambers because they was waiting for new arrivals, so they needed the room.
And then it take me-- it took me a while.
I started to learn to crawl, from crawling standing up-- like a child-- and slowly my eyesight came together, and slowly my earsight came together.
And just because I was so protected by those girls-- because I helped them, they helped me.
And this was-- I was always saying to the girls, we have to help each other.
We haven't got anybody who, who wants us to be alive.
Just we want to be-- us to alive.
There was a young girl, who was much younger, like I am. and she was blond with blue eyes, and so was her sister.
They was very young girls.
And she said to me-- she was speaking-- she was from a-- like a peasant.
She looked like a peasant and she acted like a peasant because she came from a little village where only one family was Jewish.
So she was exactly like the other peasant girls, but she spoke a peasantly Slovak and Jewish.
So she asked me, tell me, Magda.
Are we going to survive, or we are not going survive?
And she said in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH], Magdala, like you are a clever girl.
So tell me.
I said, we are going to survive.
How you know?
I know because we are helping each other.
And if we help each other, whatever it happens, they can't finish us all.
So-- but how you know?
I said, because always some people survive by any circumstances and we will be the-- who help each other, we will survive.
But how you know?
So I said, you know how I know?
Did you hear the story about the Noah ark?
So, what was the story of Noah ark?
So I said that some animals and some people survived with him together.
So now I believe you, she said.
That's, that's how it was.
And really, we helped each other how we could.
We stealed for each other.
Somebody could steal.
I never could.
They would catch me because I was so not capable of stealing.

But there was a space that-- in the camp was a fence and like a-- and under this fence was Polish, non-Jewish woman working for the kitchen, peeling potatoes, peeling onions, peeling this, peeling that.
So some of the girls went there and with eye language shout, throw something by accident to the end of the fence.
And she did.
She always leave a few potatoes, a few onion, a few, I don't know, whatever to the fence.
And then in the evening when it was quiet, one of the girls, who was very smart, she ran and put her fingers on that, and she pulled everything out, and then she cooked.
That made a certain bond with that Polish woman with us.
She always helped us, and we became with her friends.
What happened one day-- just to show the story-- I am coming to the [NON ENGLISH], that means to the hospital-- and going with some girls who needed some attention.
After I recovered to the typhoid, I still was in the position of helper, so that's why I took some people there.
And there I see sitting like that, hunched, a girl.
And I look at her, and that was the Polish woman, Stasja.
And I said, Stasja, what happened to you?
She said, I am very sick.
I have typhus.
So I said, and what is your [POLISH]-- that was, in Polish, your friends-- all the Polish woman?
Don't they help you?
She said, till I was healthy, they wanted everything, and I gave them, and now nobody helps me.
So I said, don't worry, Stasja.
You are coming with me home, and we girls will look after you, and only to the counting you will be-- going to your barrack.
Otherwise all the time you are with us, and we will do utmost best for you.
Why was she there at the camp?
She was a Polish prisoner.
A prisoner.
They-- you don't have to be political.
There were some political, but some not political.
No.
That was many, many Polish woman there.
But then one day when she feel-- felt so much better and she was so grateful, so she started to cry.
So I said, why are you crying, Stasja?
And she said, you know, that shows you.
When we been home-- and because the priest, they stirred us up against the Jews, that the Jews killed the Jesus Christ and, and the Jews are needing blood for Passover, and, and lies like that.
But we believed and our parents believed, and so they stirred us up against the Jews.
And so we went, and we had matches.
And so when we saw a man with a beard, so we strike the match and burned his beard or burned his, his clothes.
And so then we had that big laugh-- ha ha ha-- because they was in-- terrible distraught.
And now you see, now it shows that who what we did to the Jews, and who saved me is the Jews.
So I am so grateful to you.
And if I survive, I will tell the priest off and all the others that you are handling Jews in a wrong way.
They are peace-loving, nice people, and they want to be friends with us.
Then don't make them trouble.
So this was just one case.
And then my life went on.
And there was one girl, a Jewish girl, very capable.
And because the roll calls the German couldn't do, and she was a highly intelligent girl, so they put her in charge as report writer-- that, that means the report how many people are-- and--
Beg pardon?
Because from beginning she, she got befriended with [NON ENGLISH] German woman.
You know, we, we lived next to each other, so-- and so she found out.
And she had influence, because she was in, in a position.
So she had the influence to influence her that that is a clever woman, and put her in charge.
And they did put her in charge.
But--
Privileges?
Anyway, she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably she-- I don't know.
I don't know if she had.
But, but she did very good things.
One-- I'll just show you a sample, how we helped each other.
They made-- came the SS man, and she had to be present of the selection.
And the German punctuality, every person had a dossier about his life.
And also, if, if they went to the crematorium, they wrote there that she died on heart attack.
She died pneumonia.
But-- you know?
So they had a dossier on every single person there?
Every person.
It was their office-- which one stage I was working in it, too.
But, but then there was the selection, and he selected three hundred woman.
People were standing, and he-- they had to walk.
And he said left--
The SS man.
No.
No.
There were so many.
They had the right to do it.
So he said left or right, left or right.
So those who was left, they went back to the barrack, and those who was right, they supposed to be sent to the crematoria.
And I just was passing by, and I see that-- Katka is her name-- she has the dossier, and she makes marks and marks and marks and marks.
And when she finish, she sent three hundred woman back to the barrack.
I didn't know how she do it till after the war, when I visited her in Prague.
She lived in Prague.
And I said-- because I saw that a few time, and it was a, a mystery to me.
So, so I said, Katka, how did you do it?
You see that we had a dossier.
So I took the dossier of the death people, you know, and there I made flick, flick, flick, flick.
And he went away, anyway, and he couldn't give a damn.
He didn't control if they went to the gas chamber.
There was cases that they did, but in most cases--
It didn't interest them.
They, they, certain, certain SS men didn't.
And because those was the selection during the day-- and so she said, so I made notes on this in case he saw.
He didn't come to check for paper.
He saw me writing.
But-- so I sent the people back.
But she was-- she knew me from the first minute.
And the news travel fast, so she heard how I care for people and how I explain to people, and how I tried to help people.
So when she was in charge, so she put me in charge of a barrack.
And so I had-- I was here two, three weeks, then she pulls me out, and she puts me somewhere else.
So she kept changing the barracks you were in charge of.
I tell you why.
Because according what nationality came in-- and there came in non-Jewish nationality.
Like, it came in Czech non-Jewish people there, and there was the whole family of the president, you know?
So Magda, you are going to take the Czechs.
So she put me there in charge.
Now, I looked after them, that, that it should be running properly, the barrack, that they should have the food, the drink, the bread, the butter.
The barrack should be clean.
And so much so that a lot of people had diarrhea, and those who was living on the third bunk, till they came down, it was impossible.
So-- as it doesn't sound nice to talk about it, but they finished themselves up in the dish, which was their food dish, and then they threw the, the excrement--
-- yeah-- through the, the window, because the window wasn't-- it wasn't glass in it.
It was just a frame.
Glass wasn't in it.
So-- and when I saw it-- and I was in charge.
So I went and I said, how could I help that situation?
So I went to the end of the camp, and I found out that there are some clay which is similar like making bricks from.
So-- and because they were strong woman, the Polish-- it was in the beginning, but some caught the sickness very quickly.
So I said, [INAUDIBLE], Stasja, [INAUDIBLE]-- what their names were-- we are going to do something.
We are going to disinfect the barrack.
Because when the sun came up, and all that excrement was there, so that stank hell.
And also that brings bacteria, and inside too.
So I arranged that, because I, I tell you what.
I, I don't know how I could, but I, I did.
I stopped there a man who brought things for the commandant woman with the horse and buggy.
So I said, tomorrow you are going to bring me whitewash, two barrels of whitewash.
What do you think?
Two barrels?
Where do I take?
I don't care where you take, but bring me.
But how could I bring you, because I carry that to the commandant woman?
You will [POINTS] put the barrels here, and we will be waiting for you and we will quickly take the barrels away.
If they catch me, [POINTING] I am responsible.
I tell you, I don't know where he got the whitewash, but he brought two barrels of whitewash.
And I brought the earth from the back of the camp, and at night with the girls-- because workers have been always there.
So there was a ladder, there was a stick, there was a, a bucket.
There was always tools for the worker, because after all, it was always something to do.
So we brought those things here, and some girls went on the ladder and with a stick.
And they put some rags on it, and they covered the outside and some covered the inside.
And also the windows they put with the white-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whitewash to disinfect it.
So we been very happy.
But in the morning, when the SS woman on the charge, they saw it.
So they said, we are going to hang her.
We are going to shoot her.
We are going to, to tear her to pieces, because [POINTING] she did it on spite to give sign to those pilots-- the American pilot-- that here is a camp.
And Katka, who was the report writer, she came to tell me.
And she said, what are you doing, you stupid fool?
They are going to hang you, and shoot you, and-- I don't know what they are going to do with you.
So I said, I did it for hygiene.
But how dare you?
How can you afford to do it, be in a camp and-- you, you will pay for it, so I will pay for it.
But what-- in one stage, I was a helper in a barrack, and there the girls liked me very much.
I don't know where they took the material-- satin and so-- and they made me two dolls with long feet, long hair, and a face, and with a-- I don't know how you call those dolls.
It was here, and pompon here.
A hat.
And, and silk, black silk.
And they put it on my bed.
And I thought to myself, if they found that on my bed, that this is-- where did you get that?
How did you get-- so crematorium.
But I said to myself-- I went to that SS man who was in charge in our barrack.
And I said, you probably go to home for holy day, and I was thinking that-- how about if you take that as a present to your wife?
That's wonderful.
I broke my head.
He said, what?
To take her?
But this is something terrific-- beautiful satin and, and beautiful face, white with-- terrific.
But I wasn't already in this barrack because I was somewhere else.
But I looked him up and I said, I tell you, I didn't do it.
I didn't think that I'm clever enough to give a sign for the Americans or English pilots.
I did it for hygienic reason.
And I said, it's not only for the prisoners.
I did it for you and for all the SS men who are coming here.
They catch the typhus the same way, like-- because it's the same atmosphere.
And he said, you know what, I tell you very quietly.
Recently, only 5,000 SS men died on typhus.
I thought myself, good riddance of bad rubbish.
That's what I thought myself.
And he said, hold on.
And he went to the main commanders, and he told them she did it for the good.
Because we are going there, I can catch typhus, and, and [INAUDIBLE] can die.
And this can die, the same like the other 5,000.
So the commanders agreed.
They brought me forwards-- so no, not the woman, but the man-- and they put me in charge.
I should go and tell every barrack to do the same.
They nearly killed me.
Are you crazy?
Because you always said such a crazy idea, now we have to do that anyway.
But that Polish woman-- [INAUDIBLE], her name I remember-- she was a terrible, sadistic woman.
She stirred up the woman commanders.
What, is she going to-- you are going to listen to what the man says?
You are in charge.
So OK.
So they-- then they sent me from punishment to the new experimental barrack.
[POINTING] Block 10 was a men's camp.
So my life went from here to there, from there to here, from here to there.
And--
Never--
Never a dull moment.
The experimental barrack, they-- some German doctors, they wanted to save themselves from Siberia and to the front, so they used their doctor knowledge.
And they wanted to make experiments on human which, which was-- didn't cost them anything, and they said that for the good of the German nation.
And so they established there a-- a barrack which was the only woman barrack in the men's camp.
And it was-- the windows was woodened up so we shouldn't have contact.
And, and there they did the experiments.
And way I, I told you yesterday.
When I arrived there, because I had very blonde hair-- and being in the rain and, and-- in the rain and in the sunshine, so my hair was very bleached-- I looked really very blonde.
And I was skinny, so it-- I looked taller than I am, and also my bones shriveled through arthritis, by the way.
So he said, you can't be Jewish.
It is a mistake.
I said, I am Jewish, like all others.
No.
You can't be.
Why do you think I can't be?
Because I think that we must be mistake.
So he said, tell me what you are from profession.
I said, I'm a kindergarten teacher.
I told you there is, is a mistake.
You are not Jewish, and that's it.
So then I said to myself, how about using it for the good of the people?
So I said, Hauptsturmfuhrer Dr.
Wirths-- his name I remember well-- if you want here a hospital, then we need like linen-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Keep going.
--sheets.
Sheet.
We need sheets, pillows, towels, soap.
So he said, that's all right.
And also nighties.
So he-- and I told myself, they are going to kill me for it.
He didn't.
He said, all the things you are getting, but the nighties they have wait one day or two.
So the girls was ecstatic.
Where do they get pillows, sheets, blankets, proper clean towels?
So-- And these were the girls that were being experimented on.
They, they just started.
It was the start, because they had started the barrack.
